text.title: Communication Planning and Elicitation Preparation
text.summary: Audience analysis, message design, channel selection with feedback mechanisms, and elicitation technique selection by information type.

rule.statement: Map every communication channel to a feedback mechanism; a channel without a feedback mechanism is write-only and decays within weeks.
rule.rationale: "Email weekly digest" without a reply expectation or read-confirmation mechanism produces a false sense of stakeholder alignment; unresolved misunderstandings surface as surprise issues at sign-off.

list.item: Audience analysis: who needs what information, preferred format, preferred channel, frequency requirements.
Message design: key points, level of detail appropriate for audience, supporting materials.
Channel selection: formal vs informal, written vs verbal, synchronous vs asynchronous.
Feedback mechanism: how to confirm understanding, follow-up process, issue resolution path.

example.note: Sponsor: weekly status summary by email, feedback mechanism = standing 15-minute review slot. Dev team: detailed requirements per sprint via Jira, feedback mechanism = refinement session questions log. Ops: release plan checklist pre-release via Slack, feedback mechanism = acknowledgment reaction + blockers thread.

antipattern.description: Communication plan that defaults all channels to Slack regardless of audience or message formality.
antipattern.reason: Slack is appropriate for async informal communication; regulatory-track approvals, formal sign-offs, and sponsor status reports require traceable channels with retention guarantees.

rule.statement: Define elicitation scope (what information is needed, what decisions will be made, what is out of scope) before scheduling any session.
rule.rationale: Undefined scope turns sessions into open-ended discussions that produce inconsistent outputs and require rework to extract useful requirements.

rule.statement: Pre-commit the technique-selection table per session; agents otherwise default to "interview" for all information types.
rule.rationale: Each information type has an optimally matched technique; always interviewing leaves process gaps, current-state artifacts, and validation results under-covered.

list.item: Current state information: Observation, Document Analysis.
Pain points and stakeholder needs: Interviews, Focus Groups.
New requirements and consensus: Workshops, Prototyping.
Validation of existing requirements: Reviews, Walkthroughs.

antipattern.description: LLM generates a communication plan with a six-tier steering committee for a four-person project.
antipattern.reason: LLMs over-template governance artifacts for any project they perceive as "enterprise" regardless of actual team size. Pass team size and compliance flag explicitly in the system prompt to constrain output appropriately.

antipattern.description: LLM auto-closes CRs when CI is green without requiring human approval.
antipattern.reason: Green CI indicates technical quality; governance integrity requires a human approval trace. Automating CR closure on CI signals removes the accountable human from the audit trail.

rule.statement: Require three human-in-the-loop checkpoints: (1) sign-off on the decision-authority matrix before any requirements are baselined; (2) sponsor confirmation of comms cadence; (3) post-kickoff dry-run of the CR flow with one synthetic CR.
rule.rationale: Governance integrity depends on human approval traces at critical junctures; agent-only setup produces a governance document that satisfies process checkers but fails the first real decision.

text.reference: Bash script generating governance.md skeleton under .aidocs/in-progress/.
